aliases: Pasta with chicken and mushroom in tomato sauce

english: Pasta with chicken and mushroom in tomato sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'pasta dish' class (FOODON_00002473); no specific 'pomodoro pasta' or 'pasta with tomato sauce and chicken' term exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers cooked pasta as a broad category. No specific pomodoro pasta with chicken and mushroom composite code exists in FoodEx2; closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for pomodoro pasta identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser; set to null.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pomodoro pasta is approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as commonly served in Singapore food courts and casual dining establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Pomodoro Pasta — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pomodoro Pasta (cr_id F05060098), which reports values per 100 g of the cooked dish including pasta, fresh tomato sauce, chicken, and mushroom. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta, cooked (white, boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 49 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white pasta, cooked (boiled), which consistently reports GI in the range 45–55 (low category). The input record cited GI 49 from an embedded GI database consistent with this range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 15.3 × 2.5) / 100 = 18.7 for a 250 g serving (38.25 g carbohydrate). This is an analogous estimate; the exact composite dish (pasta + tomato sauce + chicken + mushroom) has not been independently tested in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 254 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 635 mg sodium, representing 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, limit portion to 150–200 g, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the sodium load; choosing wholemeal pasta further supports blood pressure management through increased fibre and micronutrient density.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 15.3 g/100 g with a GI of 49 (low); a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 38 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~18.7, which is in the medium-to-high GL range and requires portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the serving to approximately 150 g cooked pasta, substitute wholemeal or legume-based pasta to lower GI and increase fibre, and combine with the chicken and vegetable components to slow gastric emptying and blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is low at 1.94 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat approximately 0.4 g/100 g; tomato sauce provides lycopene, a carotenoid antioxidant associated with reduced LDL oxidation; sodium at 254 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pomodoro pasta is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet given its low fat and saturated fat content and lycopene-rich tomato base; individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake by requesting low-salt preparation and using extra-virgin olive oil to maximise oleocanthal and monounsaturated fat benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato provides lycopene and vitamin C; olive oil contributes oleocanthal with COX-inhibiting anti-inflammatory properties; trans fat is absent and saturated fat is negligible.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may generally consume pomodoro pasta as part of an anti-inflammatory Mediterranean-style diet, but those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor symptom response to tomato and consider substituting with a non-nightshade sauce if symptoms worsen.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pomodoro pasta (Italian: pasta al pomodoro) is a classic Italian pasta dish consisting of cooked pasta — typically spaghetti, penne, or fusilli — served with a fresh tomato-based sauce. In the Singapore food-service context, this dish commonly includes chicken and mushroom as protein and umami additions, making it a more substantial meal. The tomato sauce is prepared by sautéing garlic and onion in olive oil, adding fresh or canned tomatoes, and simmering until reduced; fresh basil is typically added at the end. The dish has a mildly tangy, savoury flavour profile with a soft pasta texture and a light, slightly oily sauce coating. Per 100 g, the dish provides 95 kcal, 4.1 g protein, 1.94 g fat, and 15.3 g carbohydrate, with 2.5 g dietary fibre and 254 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 49 (low), making it a moderate glycemic load food at typical serving sizes. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium content for hypertension management and carbohydrate load for Type 2 Diabetes; the low saturated fat and lycopene content are cardiovascular benefits. The dish is halal-suitable and gluten-containing. It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual dinner in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pomodoro pasta?
Is pasta al pomodoro suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of pasta with tomato sauce?
Is pomodoro pasta high in sodium?
Can I eat pasta with tomato sauce if I have hypertension?
What nutrients are in tomato pasta?
Is pasta with chicken and mushroom in tomato sauce good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060098) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 49 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database range for cooked white pasta (45–55). All mandatory fields are populated from approved sources. Saturated fat estimated at 0.4 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable pasta dishes with tomato sauce and chicken, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is the only estimated field.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Pasta with tomato sauce and meat' as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish; this is a conservative estimate and may be revised when HPB publishes full fatty acid data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this Singapore-specific composite preparation. 3. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this dish and no exact-match approved source was available to supply them for this specific Singapore composite preparation. 4. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in this dish type per HPB and USDA data for comparable pasta dishes with tomato sauce. 5. GI glycemic load per serving calculated for a 250 g standard plate: (49 × 38.25) / 100 = 18.7 (medium GL). 6. The input record listed 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag; however, the dish description explicitly includes chicken, making it non-vegetarian. The dietary_flags have been corrected accordingly. 7. The input health_suitability condition 'diabetes' has been mapped to the required 'Type 2 Diabetes' (machine key: type2_diabetes) per Veritas naming rules.
